drugClasses: Dopamine precursor with peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor

card.indications: Treatment of Parkinson disease and parkinsonian syndromes when symptoms improve with levodopa. Carbidopa reduces peripheral levodopa breakdown, allowing more levodopa to reach the brain and reducing nausea and other peripheral effects.
card.mechanism: Levodopa crosses the blood-brain barrier and is converted to dopamine in the central nervous system. Carbidopa inhibits peripheral dopa-decarboxylase, decreasing levodopa conversion outside the brain and increasing the amount available to the CNS.
card.dosing: Dosing is individualized according to symptoms, prior levodopa exposure, formulation, and adverse effects. Immediate-release therapy commonly begins with a low-strength tablet several times daily and is titrated gradually. Extended-release and enteral products use different schedules and are not directly interchangeable milligram for milligram without clinical guidance.
card.routeAdministration: Give on a consistent schedule. Swallow extended-release tablets whole; do not crush or chew. Orally disintegrating tablets dissolve on the tongue. Enteral suspension requires the designated pump and tube system. Abrupt discontinuation should be avoided because severe rigidity, fever, mental-status changes, and autonomic instability may occur.
card.contraindications: Use with nonselective monoamine oxidase inhibitors within 14 days; hypersensitivity to either component. Use caution in narrow-angle glaucoma, severe cardiovascular disease, psychosis, melanoma history, or conditions worsened by involuntary movement.
card.warnings: May cause sudden sleep episodes, hallucinations, confusion, orthostatic hypotension, dyskinesias, impulse-control disorders, and worsening motor fluctuations. Withdrawal or rapid dose reduction can produce a neuroleptic-malignant-like syndrome. Levodopa can activate malignant melanoma; monitor suspicious skin lesions.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, dyskinesia, headache, dry mouth, constipation, vivid dreams, drowsiness, confusion, and darkening of urine or sweat.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe dyskinesia; hallucinations or psychosis; sudden sleep attacks; arrhythmias; severe orthostatic hypotension or syncope; impulse-control disorders; neuroleptic-malignant-like syndrome after abrupt withdrawal; and gastrointestinal bleeding in susceptible patients.
card.interactions: High-protein meals can reduce or delay levodopa absorption. Iron salts may bind levodopa and reduce absorption. Dopamine-blocking antipsychotics and some antiemetics may reduce effectiveness. Antihypertensives can increase orthostatic hypotension. Nonselective MAO inhibitors are contraindicated.
card.monitoring: Assess mobility, rigidity, tremor, gait, swallowing, activities of daily living, timing of on/off periods, dyskinesias, blood pressure lying and standing, mental status, sleepiness, hallucinations, and impulse-control changes. Monitor weight, nutrition, skin, and cardiac status when risk factors are present.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly on schedule and do not stop suddenly. Rise slowly. Avoid driving until the effects on alertness are known. Report sudden sleep episodes, hallucinations, severe involuntary movements, fainting, compulsive gambling or spending, sexual urges, binge eating, fever, severe rigidity, or confusion. Keep a symptom and dose-timing record if requested.
card.nutrition: Take consistently in relation to meals. If nausea occurs, a small low-protein snack may help. Large or high-protein meals can delay or reduce absorption, so protein may need to be distributed evenly through the day. Separate iron supplements from doses when directed and maintain adequate fluids and fiber.

card.indications: Treatment of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, left-ventricular dysfunction after myocardial infarction, and hypertension. It reduces cardiac workload and may improve survival in selected patients with heart failure or post-MI ventricular dysfunction.
card.mechanism: Blocks beta1, beta2, and alpha1 receptors. This lowers heart rate and contractility, decreases renin release, and causes peripheral vasodilation, reducing blood pressure and myocardial oxygen demand.
card.dosing: Start low and titrate slowly. Heart-failure therapy commonly begins with 3.125 mg twice daily and may be doubled at intervals as tolerated. Hypertension commonly begins at 6.25 mg twice daily. Extended-release products are dosed once daily and are not substituted without dose-conversion guidance.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food to slow absorption and reduce orthostatic effects. Check pulse and blood pressure before administration according to facility parameters. Do not stop abruptly, especially in patients with coronary disease. Extended-release capsules should be swallowed whole; some products may be opened and sprinkled on soft food if allowed by labeling.
card.contraindications: Severe bradycardia, second- or third-degree AV block or sick-sinus syndrome without a pacemaker, cardiogenic shock, decompensated heart failure requiring IV inotropes, severe hepatic impairment, and bronchial asthma or related bronchospastic conditions.
card.warnings: Can worsen heart failure during initiation or titration, cause bradycardia, heart block, hypotension, bronchospasm, and mask hypoglycemia. Abrupt withdrawal may precipitate angina, myocardial infarction, or arrhythmias. Use caution in peripheral vascular disease and diabetes.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, fatigue, low blood pressure, slow pulse, diarrhea, weight gain, weakness, and mild edema.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Heart block, severe bradycardia, syncope, cardiogenic shock, worsening heart failure, severe bronchospasm, serious hypotension, hepatic injury, and severe hypoglycemia that may be harder to recognize.
card.interactions: Other heart-rate-lowering drugs such as digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, amiodarone, and clonidine can increase bradycardia or conduction problems. Antihypertensives and alcohol may increase hypotension. CYP2D6 inhibitors may raise carvedilol exposure. Beta blockers may alter insulin response and mask hypoglycemia symptoms.
card.monitoring: Monitor pulse, blood pressure, daily weight, edema, lung sounds, dyspnea, fatigue, heart-failure symptoms, glucose in diabetic patients, and signs of poor perfusion. During titration, assess orthostatic blood pressure and worsening fluid retention.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food at the same times daily. Do not stop suddenly. Check pulse and blood pressure if instructed. Rise slowly and report fainting, wheezing, swelling, rapid weight gain, worsening shortness of breath, or a very slow pulse. Diabetic patients should monitor glucose carefully because warning symptoms may be masked.
card.nutrition: Take with food. Follow prescribed sodium and fluid guidance for heart failure or hypertension. Avoid excessive alcohol because it can worsen dizziness and hypotension. Track daily weight consistently and report rapid gain.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible respiratory, ear, skin and soft-tissue, bone, and genitourinary infections. It should be used only for infections proven or strongly suspected to be bacterial.
card.mechanism: Binds to penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis, causing cell lysis and death in susceptible organisms.
card.dosing: Adult dosing commonly ranges from 250 mg every 6 hours to 500 mg every 12 hours, depending on infection type and severity. More severe infections may require higher total daily doses. Pediatric dosing is weight based. Renal impairment requires dose adjustment.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Shake oral suspension well and measure with an accurate device. Space doses evenly and complete the full prescribed course. Store reconstituted suspension according to the product instructions, commonly refrigerated, and discard after the labeled period.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to cephalexin or other cephalosporins. Use caution with a history of immediate severe penicillin allergy because cross-reactivity is possible.
card.warnings: Serious allergic reactions, Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea, seizures with excessive accumulation, and superinfection may occur. Dose reduction is important in renal impairment. Positive direct Coombs tests and altered glucose tests may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, dyspepsia, rash, itching, and headache.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, severe skin reactions, C. difficile colitis, hemolytic anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, seizures, liver injury, and interstitial nephritis.
card.interactions: Probenecid can increase cephalexin concentrations. Metformin exposure may increase. Anticoagulants may have enhanced effect in some patients. Live bacterial vaccines may be less effective during antibiotic therapy.
card.monitoring: Assess infection symptoms, culture results when available, allergy history, bowel pattern, hydration, renal function, and clinical response. Monitor for rash, wheezing, facial swelling, persistent diarrhea, superinfection, and neurologic changes in renal impairment.
card.patientTeaching: Take every dose and finish the prescription even if symptoms improve. Report trouble breathing, facial swelling, severe rash, watery or bloody diarrhea, or persistent vomiting. Do not use leftover antibiotics or share them. Measure liquid doses accurately.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if stomach upset occurs. Maintain hydration, especially with fever or diarrhea. Food does not significantly reduce effectiveness. Report prolonged diarrhea rather than treating it independently with antidiarrheals.

dosageForms: Orally disintegrating forms in selected products

card.indications: Temporary relief of allergic rhinitis symptoms such as sneezing, runny nose, itchy nose or throat, and itchy watery eyes. Also used for urticaria and itching when prescribed.
card.mechanism: Selectively blocks peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated itching, sneezing, nasal drainage, and hives.
card.dosing: Adults and children 6 years and older commonly take 5 to 10 mg once daily, depending on symptom severity and product labeling. Younger children use age-specific liquid dosing. Lower or less frequent dosing may be needed in kidney or liver impairment.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Measure liquid carefully. Do not exceed the labeled daily dose. Sedation is less common than with first-generation antihistamines but can still occur.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cetirizine, hydroxyzine, levocetirizine, or product ingredients. Use only with clinician guidance in significant renal or hepatic impairment.
card.warnings: May cause drowsiness and impaired alertness. Alcohol, tranquilizers, sedatives, opioids, and other CNS depressants can intensify sedation. Rare severe itching has been reported after stopping long-term daily use.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, fatigue, dry mouth, headache, dizziness, sore throat, and mild abdominal discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypersensitivity or anaphylaxis, urinary retention in susceptible patients, severe sedation, and rare intense pruritus after discontinuation.
card.interactions: Alcohol, sedatives, tranquilizers, opioids, sleep medications, and other antihistamines can increase drowsiness. Theophylline may modestly reduce cetirizine clearance at higher doses.
card.monitoring: Assess allergy symptoms, hives, itching, sedation, fall risk, renal and liver history, and response to therapy. Monitor for paradoxical agitation in children and urinary difficulty in patients with retention risk.
card.patientTeaching: Do not drive or operate machinery until you know how the medication affects you. Avoid alcohol and combining it with sedating products unless approved. Use only the recommended dose. Seek care for breathing difficulty, facial swelling, or widespread hives.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration for dry mouth. Alcohol should be avoided because it increases drowsiness. No specific food restrictions are required.

dosageForms: Oral concentrate or syrup in selected products

card.indications: Treatment of schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, severe behavioral disturbance, severe nausea and vomiting, intractable hiccups, preoperative apprehension, and selected manic states. Use depends on formulation and indication.
card.mechanism: Blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the brain and also has anticholinergic, antihistamine, and alpha-adrenergic blocking effects. These actions reduce psychosis and nausea but also account for many adverse effects.
card.dosing: Dosing varies widely by indication, age, severity, and route. Oral psychiatric dosing often begins low and is increased gradually. IM doses are used for acute situations under close monitoring. Older or debilitated patients usually require lower doses.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses consistently and with food if GI upset occurs. For IM use, administer deep into a large muscle and monitor blood pressure closely. Keep the patient supine after parenteral dosing when ordered because severe hypotension can occur. Avoid abrupt discontinuation after prolonged therapy.
card.contraindications: Coma or severe CNS depression, bone-marrow suppression, hypersensitivity to phenothiazines, and concurrent use of large amounts of CNS depressants. Use caution in seizure disorders, glaucoma, urinary retention, cardiovascular disease, liver disease, and Parkinsonism.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: increased mortality in older adults with dementia-related psychosis; not approved for that use. Major risks include neuroleptic malignant syndrome, tardive dyskinesia, severe hypotension, agranulocytosis, seizures, cholestatic jaundice, QT prolongation, and severe photosensitivity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, orthostatic hypotension, nasal congestion, weight gain, sexual dysfunction, and mild extrapyramidal symptoms.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome; tardive dyskinesia; severe dystonia or akathisia; agranulocytosis; seizures; severe hypotension; arrhythmias or QT prolongation; cholestatic jaundice; severe skin reactions; and aspiration related to sedation or dysphagia.
card.interactions: Alcohol, opioids, sedatives, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory and CNS depression. Anticholinergic drugs worsen constipation, urinary retention, and confusion. Antihypertensives increase hypotension. QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk. Levodopa and dopamine agonists may have reduced effect.
card.monitoring: Assess mental status, behavior, suicide risk, blood pressure lying and standing, temperature, rigidity, gait, swallowing, involuntary movements, bowel and bladder function, weight, glucose, lipids, CBC when indicated, liver function, and ECG in patients with risk factors.
card.patientTeaching: Rise slowly and avoid driving until effects are known. Avoid alcohol and overheating. Use sunscreen and protective clothing. Report fever, severe rigidity, confusion, fainting, palpitations, sore throat, infection, jaundice, trouble swallowing, or new involuntary movements immediately. Do not stop suddenly without guidance.
card.nutrition: Take with food if nausea occurs. Increase fluids and fiber unless contraindicated because constipation is common. Limit alcohol completely. Monitor weight and metabolic health, and avoid excessive heat because temperature regulation may be impaired.
